text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac and gastrocolic ligament, preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. The cystic lesion is identified upon entering the lesser sac. The stomach is mobilized from the antrum to the gastrosplenic ligament. A laparoscopic ultrasound is used to assess the lesion for vascular involvement, clearly identifying the borders of the mass.

The surgeon identifies and isolates the splenic artery and vein from the posterior aspect of the pancreas using both superior and inferior approaches to the retropancreatic space. The infrapancreatic border is developed, and the retroperitoneal plane is opened. The proximal transection site of the pancreas is identified at the portal vein level, achieved through alternating supra- and infrapancreatic approaches at the neck of the pancreas. The periportal region is dissected and skeletonized, with ligation of vascular connections between the pancreas and splenic vessels. A retropancreatic tunnel is created, and umbilical tape is passed to manipulate the distal pancreas. The pancreas is isolated from the splenic vasculature, and a stapling device is used to transect it. The distal staple line is excised and sent for frozen section to confirm negative margins, and the distal pancreatic duct is closed with a suture for further dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the tortuous path of the splenic vessels using a bipolar vessel sealer to prevent thermal spread and vascular injury. As the dissection approaches the splenic hilum, the pancreas is seen dropping inferiorly below the hilum, requiring delineation from the splenic vessels. The proximal staple line of the pancreas is over-sewn, and an adhesive polymer is applied to seal the new distal aspect of the pancreas.
